items.myth: A 'CLI' means a separate executable, not Python.
items.reality: Installed Python CLIs *are* executables on PATH — pip generates platform-specific wrapper scripts from `[project.scripts]`. Users don't need to know Python is underneath.
items.example: `pip install ragapp` puts a `ragapp` binary in `<venv>/bin/`; `which ragapp` shows a real file.

items.reality: `argparse.add_subparsers()` handles arbitrary sub-command trees just fine. Third-party libs are nicer ergonomically, not categorically more powerful.
items.example: `git`-style CLIs are widely built on `argparse` alone.

items.myth: Testing CLIs requires subprocess and parsing stdout.
items.reality: Call `main(['index', '--backend=faiss'])` directly in tests — most parsers accept an argv list. Capture stdout/stderr with `capsys`.
items.example:
```python
def test_index(capsys):
    assert main(['index','--backend=faiss']) == 0
    assert 'built' in capsys.readouterr().out
```

items.myth: I need a shebang line in my module for `python -m` to work.
items.reality: `python -m` doesn't care about shebangs. Shebangs only matter for files run as scripts directly (`./script.py`), and even there pip's wrappers handle this for installed commands.
items.example: `python -m ragapp.cli` works whether or not `cli.py` has `#!/usr/bin/env python`.

items.myth: Once published, CLI flags are easy to change.
items.reality: Flags are an API. Renaming `--max-tokens` to `--limit` will break scripts users wrote a year ago. Treat additions as cheap, removals as expensive, defaults as load-bearing.
items.example: Add the new name, keep the old as a deprecated alias for a release or two, warn on stderr.
